1: vayhi davar YHWH el Yonah shenit lemor: 'Then the word of the LORD came to Jonah the second time, saying.' The phrase shenit (a second time) is one of Scripture's most grace-laden words: YHWH reissues the commission to the fled prophet without reproach. There is no divine rebuke, no added condition, no revision of the mission — the same words as 1:2 follow verbatim. The 'second time' motif in Scripture regularly signals renewal after failure (the second giving of the tablets, Exod 34; Elijah's second angel-touch, 1 Kings 19:7). YHWH's persistence with failed messengers is the book's first object-lesson in divine patience.
2: qum lech el Nineveh hair hagedolah: 'Arise, go to Nineveh, that great city.' The commissioning formula repeats exactly (cf. Jonah 1:2 qum lech) — YHWH does not reword his command to suit the prophet's experience. The only addition: veqera eleiha et haqeriah asher anochi dover eleicha — 'and proclaim to it the proclamation that I tell you.' The content of the proclamation is withheld until it is actually delivered (v4) — the text models Jonah's compliance without giving him advance knowledge of what he will say.
3: vayaqam Yonah vayelech el Nineveh kidvar YHWH: 'So Jonah arose and went to Nineveh according to the word of the LORD.' The contrast with Jonah 1:3 (vayaqam Yonah livroach, he rose to flee) is exact and intentional: same verb vayaqam (rose), opposite direction. Vehineveh haita ir gedolah le'elohim mahalach sheloshet yamim — 'now Nineveh was an exceedingly great city, a three-day journey.' The unusual superlative le'elohim (to God = exceedingly, the divine elative in Hebrew) underscores the magnitude of the task. The Nineveh of Jonah's time was indeed one of the largest cities in the ancient world (confirmed by archaeology).
4: od arbaim yom veNineveh nehepachet: 'Yet forty days, and Nineveh shall be overthrown.' This is the entire content of Jonah's proclamation — five Hebrew words. The brevity is striking for a prophetic oracle. The key word is nehepachet (from hafach, H2015) — to be overturned/overthrown — the exact verb used for Sodom's destruction (Genesis 19:25; Amos 4:11). But hafach also means 'to be changed/transformed' (used of Saul's heart being changed, 1 Samuel 10:6; of the heart 'turned,' Lamentations 1:20). The narrative's resolution: Nineveh is nehepachet — transformed by repentance rather than overthrown by judgment. The ambiguity is built into the proclamation itself.
5: vaya'aminu anshei Nineveh be'elohim: 'And the people of Nineveh believed in God.' The verb vaya'aminu (believed/trusted, from aman, H539) — the root of amen — marks the greatest Gentile mass repentance in Scripture. The same verb is used of Abraham's faith in Genesis 15:6 (vehe'emin baYHWH, and he believed in YHWH, 'and it was counted to him as righteousness'). The response is immediate and comprehensive: from the greatest to the least (migadolam ad qetanam), they proclaimed a fast and put on sackcloth. The sackcloth (saq) is the formal garb of mourning and repentance.
6: vayigga hadavar el melech Nineveh: 'And the word reached the king of Nineveh.' The passive vayigga (and it reached/touched) — the word's spreading from the people to the king is natural but the formulation has an almost physical quality. The king's response is described with four verbs: he arose (vayaqam) from his throne, removed (vayyaavar) his robe, covered himself with sackcloth (vayechas saq), and sat in ashes (vayeshev al haeefer). The sequence moves from the highest status-symbol (throne) to the deepest humility-symbol (ashes) — the reversal principle that permeates the entire book.
7: al yitam adam uvehema baqar vetzon: 'Let neither man nor beast, herd nor flock, taste anything.' The royal decree extends the fast to include livestock — an unusual feature that underscores the totality of the communal response. The inclusion of animals in fasting (wearing sackcloth, Jonah 3:8) is not known from any other OT text but is attested in Greco-Roman accounts of Persian mourning practices. Whether this is narrative hyperbole for emphasis or a historical memory, it communicates the intensity and comprehensiveness of Nineveh's repentance: even the animals participate in the mourning that the prophet who fled to avoid this very event had refused to facilitate.
8: veyikasu saqim ha'adam uvehema: 'But let man and beast be covered with sackcloth.' The decree goes beyond fasting to active moral turning: veyashuvu ish miderko hara'ah umin hechamah asher bekhapeihim — 'let everyone turn from his evil way and from the violence that is in his hands.' The two terms derekh ra'ah (evil way) and chamas (violence, H2555) are the two charges leveled against the pre-flood generation (Genesis 6:5, 11) — Nineveh's repentance is using the language of the most comprehensive wickedness in biblical memory, suggesting the gravity of the sin being acknowledged.
9: mi yodea yashuv venicham ha'elohim: 'Who knows? God may turn and relent.' The king's theological formulation is remarkable: mi yodea (who knows) — not presumption but humble openness to divine mercy. The same phrase appears in Joel 2:14 (in the same repentance context) and 2 Samuel 12:22 (David hoping for the child's life). Yashuv venicham (turn and relent) — nicham (H5162) is the verb for divine relenting/repenting/changing course, used in Exodus 32:12-14 and Joel 2:13-14. The king uses the exact vocabulary of the prophetic tradition about divine relenting — how he knew this vocabulary is unexplained, which is part of Jonah's theological mystery.
10: vayar ha'elohim et maaseihem ki shavu miderech ra'atam: 'When God saw what they did, how they turned from their evil way, God relented of the disaster.' The sequence mirrors Exodus 32:14 and Joel 2:13-14: God sees the repentance and vayinachem (relented). The crucial word: velo asah — 'and he did not do it.' The disaster he had threatened did not come. This is the theological center of the book: YHWH is genuinely responsive to repentance — even Gentile repentance, even Assyrian repentance. The character formula of Exodus 34:6-7 (slow to anger, abounding in steadfast love) is at work, as Jonah himself will confirm in his complaint (Jonah 4:2).
